---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
# bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: superhero 
---

# The Negative Binomial-LogNormal model {#sec-nb-lognormal}

In @sec-grn-biophysics, we derived two complementary biophysical results that
together motivate a hierarchy of count models for single-cell RNA sequencing
data:

1. At the level of a single gene with a one-state bursty promoter and
geometrically-distributed burst sizes, the steady-state mRNA count distribution
is **exactly** Negative Binomial. This is a consequence of the chemical master
equation in the bursty limit and does not require any approximation.

2. At the level of multiple genes evolving under regulatory feedback, a Linear
Noise Approximation (LNA) of the Chemical Langevin Equation in log-abundance
coordinates yields a multivariate Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process. Its
stationary distribution is multivariate Gaussian on the log-abundances, with
covariance $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ determined by the Lyapunov equation.

Two existing scribe models specialize these results in different ways. The
Poisson-LogNormal (@sec-poisson-lognormal) treats the multivariate Gaussian as
a vector of log-Poisson rates, capturing cross-gene correlations through
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ but **discarding** the bursty nature of
transcription by collapsing the inner observation channel to Poisson shot
noise. The Logistic-Normal-Multinomial (@sec-logistic-normal-multinomial) maps
the Gaussian latent into the simplex through a softmax transform, modelling
compositional correlations conditional on a separately-modelled total count.

In this section, we introduce a third member of this family that uses **both**
biophysical results simultaneously: the **Negative Binomial-LogNormal** model.
The construction is hierarchical:

- The inner observation layer is the bursty Negative Binomial of result (1).
- The outer regulatory layer is the multivariate log-normal of result (2),
  entering as a per-cell multiplicative offset on the gene means.

We will show that this model:

1. Is a **strict generalization** of the independent Negative Binomial baseline:
setting the regulatory covariance to zero (so that $z_c = 0$ for all cells $c$)
recovers independent gene-specific NB likelihoods; under the additional
shared-$p$ constraint $\tilde{\mu}_g / r_g = \text{const}$, this further
reduces to the NBDM model of @sec-dirichlet-multinomial.

2. **Approaches** the Poisson-LogNormal in the limit $r_g \to \infty$ uniformly
across genes, in which the bursty NB collapses to its Poisson limit.

3. **Resolves** the documented failure of the Poisson-LogNormal on low-count
genes by preserving the Gamma mixing structure that gives the Negative
Binomial its characteristic spike of probability mass near zero.

4. Admits an analytically clean inference procedure: the inner Gamma layer can
be marginalized in closed form via Gamma-Poisson conjugacy, leaving a single
continuous latent $\underline{z}_c$ per cell whose log-posterior is concave.

The relationship between this model and the Poisson-LogNormal mirrors the
relationship between the Negative Binomial and the Poisson: the
NB-LogNormal is the Poisson-LogNormal corrected for transcriptional
burstiness, while preserving the Gaussian correlation structure that the
OU-at-steady-state argument justifies for upstream regulators.

## Generative model {#sec-nbln-generative}

We index cells by $c = 1, \ldots, C$ and genes by $g = 1, \ldots, G$. The
observed count of gene $g$ in cell $c$ is denoted $u_{cg} \in \mathbb{N}$. The
model is specified hierarchically by three layers.

### Latent regulatory state

Each cell carries a $G$-dimensional latent regulatory state
$\underline{z}_c \in \mathbb{R}^G$ drawn from a multivariate Gaussian:

$$
\underline{z}_c \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{0},\ \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\right),
\qquad c = 1, \ldots, C.
$${#eq-nbln-z-prior}

The covariance matrix $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} \in \mathbb{R}^{G \times G}$
is a global parameter. As established in @sec-grn-biophysics, this covariance is
the stationary solution of the Lyapunov equation associated with the linearized
log-abundance dynamics under the LNA.

### Latent expression rate

Given the regulatory state, the latent Poisson rate $\lambda_{cg}$ of gene $g$
in cell $c$ is drawn from a Gamma distribution whose mean is modulated by
$z_{cg}$:

$$
\lambda_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{Gamma}\!\left(
    \text{shape} = r_g,\
    \text{rate} = \frac{r_g}{m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)}
\right),
$${#eq-nbln-lambda-gamma}

where the conditional mean is

$$
m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c) =
\mu_{cg} \cdot \exp(z_{cg}),
\qquad
\mu_{cg} > 0.
$${#eq-nbln-mean-link}

The dispersion $r_g > 0$ is gene-specific but **independent of**
$\underline{z}_c$. This independence is a deliberate modelling choice that we
will exploit repeatedly: it is what makes the inner Gamma layer marginalizable
in closed form (@sec-nbln-marginalize) and what gives the resulting log-likelihood
its concavity properties (@sec-nbln-elbo).

The baseline mean $\mu_{cg}$ admits the standard scVI-style decomposition into a
per-cell library size $\ell_c$ and a gene-specific relative abundance
$\tilde{\mu}_g$:

$$
\log \mu_{cg} = \log \ell_c + \log \tilde{\mu}_g.
$${#eq-nbln-mean-decomposition}

In our framework, $\ell_c$ is **not** absorbed into $z_c$; it is modelled
separately through the capture-prior construction of @sec-capture-prior, which
ensures that $\underline{z}_c$ retains its interpretation as a latent
**regulatory** state rather than a junk drawer for technical noise. We return
to this point in @sec-nbln-comparison.

### Observation

Conditional on the latent rate, the count is Poisson:

$$
u_{cg} \mid \lambda_{cg} \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_{cg}).
$${#eq-nbln-poisson}

### Graphical model

The full joint distribution is

$$
\pi(\underline{\underline{U}},
    \underline{\underline{\Lambda}},
    \underline{\underline{Z}})
=
\prod_{c=1}^C
\pi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\prod_{g=1}^G
\pi(\lambda_{cg} \mid z_{cg}, r_g, \mu_{cg})\,
\pi(u_{cg} \mid \lambda_{cg}),
$${#eq-nbln-joint}

with $\underline{\underline{U}} \in \mathbb{N}^{C \times G}$ the observed count
matrix, $\underline{\underline{\Lambda}} \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^{C \times G}$ the
matrix of latent rates, and $\underline{\underline{Z}} \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times G}$
the matrix of regulatory states. The structure is depicted below.

```{.tikz caption="Graphical model for the Negative Binomial-LogNormal. White nodes are latent variables, gray nodes are observed variables, and plates denote replication." label="fig-nbln-graphical-model" additionalPackages="\\usetikzlibrary{positioning,fit,backgrounds}"}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=stealth,
  node distance=10mm and 12mm,
  latent/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=1pt},
  obs/.style={circle, draw, fill=gray!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=1pt},
  param/.style={rectangle, draw, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=2pt},
  plate/.style={draw, rounded corners, inner sep=4mm}
]
  % Globals
  \node[param] (Sigma) {$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$};
  \node[param, right=of Sigma, xshift=-10mm] (mu) {$\tilde{\mu}_g$};
  \node[param, right=of mu, xshift=-10mm] (rg) {$r_g$};

  % Cell-level
  \node[latent, below=of Sigma, yshift=1mm] (z) {$\underline{z}_c$};
  \node[latent, right=of z, xshift=-5mm] (lam) {$\lambda_{cg}$};
  \node[obs, below=of lam, yshift=7mm] (u) {$u_{cg}$};

  % Edges
  \draw[->] (Sigma) -- (z);
  \draw[->] (z) -- (lam);
  \draw[->] (mu) -- (lam);
  \draw[->] (rg) -- (lam);
  \draw[->] (lam) -- (u);

  % Gene plate
  \begin{scope}[on background layer]
    \node[plate, fit=(lam)(u), label={[yshift=-4mm,xshift=4mm]north east:$g=1,\ldots,G$}] (geneplate) {};
  \end{scope}

  % Cell plate
  \begin{scope}[on background layer]
    \node[plate, fit=(z)(geneplate), label={[yshift=-3mm,xshift=0mm]north east:$c=1,\ldots,C$}] (cellplate) {};
  \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
```

## Marginalizing the inner Gamma layer {#sec-nbln-marginalize}

The model as written has two latent quantities per observation: the regulatory
state $z_{cg}$ and the rate $\lambda_{cg}$. The first is a continuous,
correlated latent that we must approximate variationally. The second can be
**marginalized analytically** through Gamma-Poisson conjugacy, dramatically
simplifying inference.

### Gamma-Poisson conjugacy

Recall (cf. @sec-dirichlet-multinomial) that if
$\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\text{shape} = r,\ \text{rate} = \beta)$ and
$u \mid \lambda \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, then the marginal distribution
of $u$ is Negative Binomial in the $(r, p)$ parameterization with
$p = \beta / (\beta + 1)$, or equivalently in the (mean, dispersion)
parameterization with mean $r/\beta$ and dispersion $r$. Specifically,

$$
\pi(u \mid r, \beta)
=
\int_0^\infty
\text{Poisson}(u \mid \lambda)\,
\text{Gamma}(\lambda \mid r, \beta)\,
\mathrm{d}\lambda
=
\text{NB}\!\left(u \mid r,\ p = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1}\right).
$${#eq-nbln-gamma-poisson}

### Application to the conditional likelihood

For the NB-LogNormal model, conditional on $\underline{z}_c$, equations
@eq-nbln-lambda-gamma and @eq-nbln-poisson have the form of @eq-nbln-gamma-poisson
with shape $r_g$ and rate $\beta_{cg} = r_g / m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)$. Applying
the conjugacy result gives

$$
u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{NB}\!\left(
    \text{mean} = m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c),\
    \text{dispersion} = r_g
\right),
$${#eq-nbln-conditional-nb}

with the corresponding success probability

$$
\boxed{\
p_g(z_{cg}) =
\frac{r_g}{r_g + \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}}
\ }.
$${#eq-nbln-pg-z}

### Linear dependence in logit space

The conditional NB likelihood depends on $\underline{z}_c$ exclusively through
the success probability $p_g(z_{cg})$. On the logit scale, this dependence is
**linear**:

$$
\text{logit}\bigl(p_g(z_{cg})\bigr) =
\log\frac{r_g}{\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}} =
\log r_g - \log \mu_{cg} - z_{cg}.
$${#eq-nbln-logit-linear}

Equivalently, on the failure-logit scale (the convention used by most
probabilistic-programming libraries for the Negative Binomial),

$$
\log\frac{1 - p_g(z_{cg})}{p_g(z_{cg})}
= \log \mu_{cg} + z_{cg} - \log r_g.
$${#eq-nbln-failure-logit}

The latent enters as a **clean additive term** in the logits.
Reparameterized gradients with respect to the variational parameters of
$q(\underline{z}_c)$ flow through this linear term without difficulty, which is
critical for stochastic variational inference (@sec-nbln-elbo).

### What does and does not depend on $\underline{z}_c$

The following table summarizes the dependence of the relevant quantities on the
latent regulatory state. The key observation is that the **dispersion**
$r_g$ is independent of $\underline{z}_c$. This is what allows the Gamma layer
to be marginalized in closed form: had the dispersion depended on
$\underline{z}_c$, the conjugacy in @eq-nbln-gamma-poisson would no longer apply.

| Quantity | Depends on $\underline{z}_c$? | Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Conditional mean $\langle u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \rangle$ | Yes | $\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}$ |
| Success probability $p_g(z_{cg})$ | Yes | $r_g / (r_g + \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}})$ |
| Conditional variance $\text{Var}(u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c)$ | Yes | $\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}} + (\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}})^2 / r_g$ |
| Dispersion $r_g$ | **No** | gene-specific constant |
| Baseline mean $\mu_{cg}$ | No | $\ell_c \tilde{\mu}_g$ |

After marginalizing $\lambda$, the model reduces to a Negative Binomial
likelihood whose log-mean is offset by a Gaussian latent:

$$
\boxed{\
u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{NB}\!\left(\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}},\ r_g\right),
\qquad
\underline{z}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}).
\ }
$${#eq-nbln-marginalized}

This is the form of the model used throughout the rest of this section.

## Biophysical interpretation {#sec-nbln-biophysics}

Having specified the model, we now justify each layer biophysically. The
argument relies entirely on results already derived in @sec-grn-biophysics; the
new content is the **synthesis** that combines them.

### Inner layer: bursty transcription gives an exact Negative Binomial

In @sec-grn-biophysics, we derived the steady-state distribution of mRNA counts
under the one-state bursty promoter model. Briefly, the gene transcribes
mRNAs in geometrically-distributed bursts at rate $k_b$, and individual mRNAs
degrade at rate $\gamma$. The chemical master equation for this system has the
exact stationary solution

$$
m \sim \text{NB}\!\left(
    r = \frac{k_b}{\gamma},\
    p = \frac{1}{1 + b}
\right),
$${#eq-nbln-bursty-nb}

where $b$ is the mean burst size. The Negative Binomial here is **not an
approximation** — it is the exact stationary distribution of a well-characterized
biophysical process. Mapping to our notation: $r_g$ corresponds to the
gene-specific burst frequency divided by the degradation rate, and the success
probability encodes the burst size.

### Outer layer: regulatory dynamics give a multivariate log-normal

Also in @sec-grn-biophysics, we derived that the joint stationary distribution
of log-abundances under coupled regulatory dynamics, after the LNA, is
multivariate Gaussian:

$$
\underline{x}^{(c)} \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{\bar{x}},\
                   \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\right),
\qquad
x_g = \log \lambda_g,
$${#eq-nbln-x-prior}

with $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ fixed by the Lyapunov equation
$\underline{\underline{J}}\,\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} +
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\,\underline{\underline{J}}^\top +
2\underline{\underline{D}} = \underline{\underline{0}}$, where
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ is the Jacobian of the linearized log-rate drift
and $\underline{\underline{D}}$ is the diffusion matrix.

Defining $\underline{z}_c = \underline{x}^{(c)} - \underline{\bar{x}}$ recenters
this to the zero-mean form @eq-nbln-z-prior used in our model.

### The synthesis: timescale separation

The two results above hold at different temporal scales:

- The bursty NB result @eq-nbln-bursty-nb is the stationary distribution under a
  fixed burst rate $k_b$. It treats the regulatory environment as constant.
- The log-normal result @eq-nbln-x-prior describes how that regulatory
  environment fluctuates across cells.

The synthesis assumes a **separation of timescales** between these two layers.
Concretely: transcription factor (TF) concentrations and pathway activities
(the slow regulatory state $\underline{z}_c$) evolve on timescales much longer
than the burst dynamics of any individual gene. Each cell, observed at a single
snapshot, has effectively reached the bursty NB stationary distribution
**conditional on its own slow regulatory state**, but different cells are
sampled from different points along the slow OU trajectory.

Under this assumption, the regulatory state perturbs the NB mean
multiplicatively while holding the dispersion $r_g$ fixed. In the one-state
bursty promoter parameterization, the NB mean is
$r_g \cdot b_g$ where $r_g = k_{i,g}/\gamma_g$ is the burst frequency and
$b_g$ is the mean burst size. Since $r_g$ is held constant in our model
(@eq-nbln-lambda-gamma), the regulatory perturbation acts on the **burst size**
parameter:

$$
b_g(\underline{z}_c) =
b_g^{(0)}\,
\exp\!\left(\underline{w}_g^\top \underline{u}_c\right),
$${#eq-nbln-burst-size}

where $\underline{u}_c \in \mathbb{R}^K$ is the vector of TF activities in
cell $c$ and $\underline{w}_g \in \mathbb{R}^K$ is the regulatory loading of
gene $g$. In a two-state promoter context, this corresponds to regulation of
the transcription initiation rate $k_{\text{tx}}$ or the promoter off-rate
$k_{\text{off}}$---mechanisms that alter how many transcripts are produced per
burst event without changing the frequency at which bursts are initiated. The
induced perturbation of the NB mean is then

$$
\mu_{cg} \to \mu_{cg} \cdot
\exp\!\left(\underline{w}_g^\top \underline{u}_c\right) =
\mu_{cg} \cdot \exp(z_{cg}),
$${#eq-nbln-mean-perturbation}

with $z_{cg} = \underline{w}_g^\top \underline{u}_c$. This is precisely the
mean-link function @eq-nbln-mean-link assumed in our model. The vector
$\underline{z}_c \in \mathbb{R}^G$ is the linear projection of a low-dimensional
TF state $\underline{u}_c \in \mathbb{R}^K$ through the loading matrix
$\underline{\underline{W}}$; if $\underline{u}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_K)$,
then $\underline{z}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top)$ exactly, motivating the low-rank structure of
@sec-nbln-low-rank.

### Comparison to PLN and LNM {#sec-nbln-comparison}

The three models built on @sec-grn-biophysics share the same outer Gaussian
layer but differ in their treatment of the inner observation channel:

| Model | Inner observation channel | Biophysical role of $\underline{z}_c$ | Linearizations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Poisson-LogNormal (@sec-poisson-lognormal) | Poisson | log-rate of expression | 1 (rate SDE) |
| Logistic-Normal-Multinomial (@sec-logistic-normal-multinomial) | Multinomial via softmax | log-composition | 1 (rate SDE) |
| **Negative Binomial-LogNormal** (this section) | **Bursty NB** | **log-modulator of expression scale (burst size)** | **2 (rate SDE + regulatory SDE)** |

The PLN and LNM models invoke a single linearization (the LNA on the rate SDE)
and obtain a Gaussian on log-rates that is fed directly into a memoryless
observation channel. The NB-LogNormal invokes an additional linearization
(of the regulatory dose-response) and uses the resulting Gaussian as a
multiplicative offset on a non-memoryless inner channel that has its own
biophysical foundation.

The cost of the second linearization is one additional assumption (timescale
separation between regulatory dynamics and burst dynamics). The benefit is
a count distribution whose **shape** matches single-cell data far more
faithfully on sparse genes (see @sec-nbln-zero-spike). Before turning to that
empirical advantage, however, we must address a tension between the biophysics
and the mathematics of the model.

### The biophysically ideal model: why it is intractable {#sec-nbln-ideal-model}

The biophysics literature on transcriptional kinetics (e.g., smFISH and MS2
live-imaging studies) consistently finds that transcription factors and
enhancers primarily regulate **burst frequency** ($k_{\text{on}}$) rather than
burst size ($b$). Under the one-state bursty promoter, $r_g = k_{i,g}/\gamma_g$
is the dimensionless burst frequency. If the GRN modulates this quantity, the
natural model would let $r_g$ depend on the regulatory state:

$$
r_{cg}(\underline{z}_c) = r_g^{(0)}\, \exp(z_{cg}),
\qquad
\underline{z}_c \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}).
$${#eq-nbln-ideal-r}

With this choice, the Gamma-Poisson marginalization still applies conditional
on $\underline{z}_c$: each gene's count is NB with cell-dependent dispersion
$r_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)$ and mean $\mu_{cg}$. The conditional log-likelihood
after marginalization is

$$
\log \text{NB}\!\left(u_{cg} \mid \mu_{cg},\, r_{cg}(z_{cg})\right) =
\log \Gamma(u_{cg} + r_{cg}) - \log \Gamma(r_{cg}) - \log(u_{cg}!)
+ r_{cg} \log p_{cg} + u_{cg} \log(1 - p_{cg}),
$${#eq-nbln-ideal-loglik}

where $p_{cg} = r_{cg} / (r_{cg} + \mu_{cg})$. This is mathematically
well-defined, but critically, **it is not log-concave in
$\underline{z}_c$**. Differentiating twice with respect to $z_{cg}$
(substituting $r_{cg} = r_g^{(0)} e^{z_{cg}}$) yields

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial z_{cg}^2} =
r_{cg}^2 \bigl[\psi'(u_{cg} + r_{cg}) - \psi'(r_{cg})\bigr]
+ r_{cg} \bigl[\psi(u_{cg} + r_{cg}) - \psi(r_{cg})\bigr]
+ r_{cg} \log p_{cg},
$${#eq-nbln-ideal-hessian}

where $\psi$ and $\psi'$ are the digamma and trigamma functions respectively.
The three terms have **competing signs**:

- The first term is negative ($\psi'$ is a decreasing function, so
  $\psi'(u + r) < \psi'(r)$).
- The second term is positive ($\psi$ is an increasing function, so
  $\psi(u + r) > \psi(r)$).
- The third term is negative ($p_{cg} < 1$, so $\log p_{cg} < 0$).

For general values of $(u_{cg}, r_{cg}, \mu_{cg})$, the sign of the sum is
**indeterminate**: the Hessian can be positive for some configurations of the
data and parameters. The per-cell log-posterior is therefore not globally
concave.

This non-concavity is not merely a nuisance — it has severe practical
consequences for inference:

1. **No unique MAP.** The per-cell log-posterior may have multiple local maxima.
   Newton iteration is not guaranteed to converge to a global optimum, and the
   Laplace approximation around a local mode may poorly represent the posterior.

2. **No well-defined Laplace approximation.** The Laplace marginal
   @eq-nbln-laplace-marginal requires the Hessian at the mode to be negative
   definite. At a saddle point or a shallow mode, the approximation degenerates.

3. **Stochastic variational inference is unreliable.** Non-convex ELBO
   landscapes with multiple local optima make gradient-based optimization
   sensitive to initialization and prone to poor solutions.

4. **The double-Woodbury machinery fails.** The efficient $O(GK + K^3)$
   Newton step of @sec-nbln-laplace relies on the Hessian being a clean
   diagonal-plus-low-rank form. When $r_g$ varies with $z$, the Hessian
   acquires a complex structure involving trigamma evaluations that does not
   admit the same factorization.

In short, the biophysically ideal model — correlation through burst
frequency — is well-defined as a generative process but **admits no scalable
inference scheme** for the per-cell latents.

### The modeling compromise {#sec-nbln-compromise}

Faced with this intractability, we make a deliberate structural choice: fix
$r_g$ as a gene-intrinsic constant and let the regulatory state modulate the
NB mean. In the bursty promoter parameterization, this is equivalent to
regulation acting on the **burst size** $b_g$ (or in the two-state promoter,
on $k_{\text{tx}} / k_{\text{off}}$) rather than on the burst frequency.

This choice is a **phenomenological compromise**, not a biophysical derivation.
Its justification is threefold:

1. **It is the unique tractable option.** As shown above, any dependence of
   $r_g$ on $\underline{z}_c$ destroys the log-concavity that the entire
   inference apparatus (Laplace, amortized VI, double-Woodbury Newton) requires.
   There is no intermediate choice between "fixed $r_g$" and "intractable."

2. **It captures the right empirical structure.** Fitted $r_g$ values from
   NB-based tools (scVI, DESeq2, edgeR) are remarkably stable across
   experimental conditions, even as gene means vary by orders of magnitude.
   Whether this reflects genuine burst-size regulation, effective averaging over
   mixed regulatory modes, or simply a useful parametric approximation to count
   overdispersion is an open question — but the empirical observation that $r_g$
   behaves as a gene-intrinsic property is well-supported.

3. **It preserves the zero-spike shape.** The critical feature the NB
   contributes over the PLN is the Gamma-mixing spike at zero for genes with
   $r_g < 1$. This spike depends only on the *value* of $r_g$, not on whether
   $r_g$ is fixed or latent. By holding $r_g$ fixed at its fitted (small) value,
   the model retains the correct marginal shape.

The NB-LogNormal is therefore best understood as a synthesis of two empirically
validated features of scRNA-seq data:

- Per-gene count distributions are overdispersed with a characteristic spike
  at zero (the NB shape, parametrized by $r_g$).
- Gene expression levels are correlated across genes in a pattern driven by
  shared regulatory programs (the multivariate Gaussian structure).

The model combines these two facts in the most tractable way: the Gaussian
modulates the NB mean while holding dispersion fixed.

### Comparison to the PLN {#sec-nbln-pln-comparison}

The Poisson-LogNormal is not more principled in this regard. The PLN applies
the Linear Noise Approximation---a continuous diffusion approximation---to
discrete bursty processes, which is formally invalid precisely for low-copy
genes where the PLN fails empirically. Both models make approximations; they
differ in where those approximations bite. The NB-LogNormal sacrifices a clean
microscopic mapping of the regulatory mechanism but gains the correct marginal
count shape. The PLN preserves the log-rate interpretation but loses the
zero-spike structure that dominates sparse genes.

## Why this resolves the PLN low-count failure {#sec-nbln-zero-spike}

The Poisson-LogNormal captures cross-gene correlations through
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, but is empirically known to mis-fit the
shape of the count distribution for low-mean, high-zero-fraction genes. In this
section we explain why the failure occurs and why the NB-LogNormal does not
inherit it.

### The mixing density at zero is what matters

Both PLN and NB-LogNormal arise from a Poisson observation channel mixed over a
latent rate distribution. The difference is the **shape of the rate
distribution near zero**.

For the Negative Binomial, the latent rate is Gamma:

$$
\pi(\lambda \mid r, \beta)
= \frac{\beta^r}{\Gamma(r)}\,
  \lambda^{r-1}\, e^{-\beta \lambda}.
$${#eq-nbln-gamma-density}

For shape $r < 1$, this density **diverges** as $\lambda \to 0^+$: it
concentrates a spike of probability mass on small rates. Convolved against
Poisson, this spike directly maps to a large fraction of zero counts.

For the Poisson-LogNormal, the latent rate is $\lambda = \exp(z)$ with
$z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, giving the log-normal density

$$
\pi(\lambda \mid \mu, \sigma)
= \frac{1}{\lambda \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}}
  \exp\!\left(
      -\frac{(\log\lambda - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
  \right).
$${#eq-nbln-lognormal-density}

This density goes to **zero** as $\lambda \to 0^+$, regardless of the values of
$\mu$ and $\sigma$. There is no spike at zero, and consequently no mechanism
for the PLN to absorb a large fraction of zero counts without distorting the
rest of its distribution.

### Moment-matching does not capture shape

Both models can match the first two moments of the count distribution. For
fixed mean $\mu$:

$$
\text{NB:}\qquad
\text{Var}(u) = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{r},
$${#eq-nbln-var-nb}

$$
\text{PLN:}\qquad
\text{Var}(u) = \mu + \mu^2 (e^{\sigma^2} - 1).
$${#eq-nbln-var-pln}

The mapping $1/r \leftrightarrow e^{\sigma^2} - 1$ matches second moments. But
the **shape** of $P(u = k)$ for small $k$ is set by the mixing density near
zero (@eq-nbln-gamma-density vs. @eq-nbln-lognormal-density), which is where the
two models structurally disagree.

### Practical consequence

To force the PLN to match an observed zero fraction near unity, the optimizer
must either drive $\mu \to -\infty$ or inflate $\sigma$. The first underestimates
expression for the cells that *do* express the gene; the second simultaneously
fattens the right tail, generating spurious very-large counts. Empirically, PLN
under-predicts the zero fraction (and over-predicts counts of one and two) for
sparse genes that the NB fits cleanly with small $r$.

The NB-LogNormal preserves the Gamma mixing density on the rate
**conditional on $\underline{z}_c$**. The cross-gene correlation enters as a
multiplicative shift in the Gamma's mean, leaving the spike-at-zero behaviour
intact for any genes with $r_g < 1$. The model thus retains the marginal
shape of the NB while gaining the cross-gene correlation structure of the PLN.

## Limiting cases {#sec-nbln-limits}

The NB-LogNormal sits between two existing scribe models, recovering each in
a clean limit. These limits provide both validation tests and intuition.

### Independent NB recovery: $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} \to 0$ {#sec-nbln-limit-nbdm}

If $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} \to \underline{\underline{0}}$ (or
equivalently, $\underline{\underline{W}} = \underline{\underline{0}}$ and
$\underline{d} = \underline{0}$ in the low-rank parameterization of
@sec-nbln-low-rank), the prior on $\underline{z}_c$ collapses to a point mass at
the origin: $\underline{z}_c = \underline{0}$ for all cells. The
marginalized likelihood @eq-nbln-marginalized then reduces to

$$
u_{cg} \sim \text{NB}(\mu_{cg}, r_g),
$${#eq-nbln-limit-nbdm-likelihood}

independently across genes. This is a product of independent gene-specific
Negative Binomials. Under the additional constraint that
$\tilde{\mu}_g / r_g = (1 - \hat{p}) / \hat{p}$ is constant across genes
(the shared-$p$ assumption), this further reduces to the NBDM model of
@sec-dirichlet-multinomial. In particular, all gene-gene correlations vanish.

This limit is useful as a **validation check**: any implementation of the
NB-LogNormal should, when initialized at $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = 0$
and trained with the regulatory parameters frozen, return likelihoods and
posterior predictive checks identical (up to numerical precision) to the
independent NB baseline.

### Poisson-LogNormal approach: $r_g \to \infty$ {#sec-nbln-limit-pln}

In the limit $r_g \to \infty$ uniformly across genes, the Gamma mixing
distribution @eq-nbln-lambda-gamma concentrates on its mean
$m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)$:

$$
\text{Gamma}\!\left(r_g,\
    \frac{r_g}{m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)}\right)
\xrightarrow{r_g \to \infty}
\delta_{m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)},
$${#eq-nbln-limit-pln-gamma}

so the latent rate becomes deterministic conditional on $\underline{z}_c$, and
the Poisson layer fires directly on $m_{cg}(\underline{z}_c) = \mu_{cg}\,
e^{z_{cg}}$. This is precisely the PLN observation model of
@sec-poisson-lognormal:

$$
u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{Poisson}\!\left(\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}\right).
$${#eq-nbln-limit-pln-likelihood}

Equivalently, in the (mean, dispersion) parameterization the conditional NB
@eq-nbln-conditional-nb tends to the Poisson with the same mean as
$r_g \to \infty$.

The two limits together establish that the NB-LogNormal interpolates
**continuously** between the independent NBDM ($\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}
\to 0$) and the Poisson-LogNormal ($r_g \to \infty$) as a function of the
regulatory covariance and the bursty dispersion. The bursty dispersion thus
controls **how much of the per-gene count variability is intrinsic** (NB-like,
spike at zero) **versus driven by extrinsic regulatory fluctuations**
(absorbed into $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$).

## Low-rank parameterization of $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ {#sec-nbln-low-rank}

For typical scRNA-seq applications with $G$ on the order of $2 \times 10^3$
to $2 \times 10^4$ genes, a full $G \times G$ covariance matrix is intractable
both statistically (too many parameters relative to the sample size) and
computationally ($O(G^3)$ for inversion). We adopt the low-rank-plus-diagonal
parameterization

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d}),
\qquad
\underline{\underline{W}} \in \mathbb{R}^{G \times K},\quad
\underline{d} \in \mathbb{R}^G_{>0},
$${#eq-nbln-low-rank}

with $K \ll G$. This is the same parameterization used by the PLN and LNM
models in scribe.

### Regulatory interpretation

Equation @eq-nbln-low-rank has a direct biophysical reading. The columns of
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ correspond to $K$ effective regulatory modules
(linear combinations of TF activities) that drive the dominant cross-gene
covariation, and $\underline{d}$ encodes the residual gene-specific intrinsic
noise that is not explained by these modules. This is precisely the
interpretation supported by the synthesis argument of
@sec-nbln-biophysics: the underlying low-dimensional TF state
$\underline{u}_c \in \mathbb{R}^K$ projects to the gene-level latent through
$\underline{z}_c = \underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{u}_c +
\underline{\epsilon}_c$ with
$\underline{\epsilon}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \text{diag}(\underline{d}))$.

### Computational benefit: Woodbury inversion

The KL term of the variational objective (@sec-nbln-elbo) requires
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}$. The Woodbury identity gives

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1} =
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1} -
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}
\left(\underline{\underline{I}}_K +
      \underline{\underline{W}}^\top
      \underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
      \underline{\underline{W}}\right)^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1},
$${#eq-nbln-woodbury}

where $\underline{\underline{D}} = \text{diag}(\underline{d})$. The right-hand
side requires only the inversion of a $K \times K$ matrix and a few
matrix-vector products, reducing the cost of computing
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1} \underline{v}$ from $O(G^3)$ to $O(K^2 G)$.
The same simplification applies to the log-determinant via the matrix-determinant
lemma:

$$
\log |\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}|
= \log |\underline{\underline{D}}|
+ \log |\underline{\underline{I}}_K +
        \underline{\underline{W}}^\top
        \underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
        \underline{\underline{W}}|.
$${#eq-nbln-logdet}

### Identifiability

The parameterization @eq-nbln-low-rank is invariant under right-multiplication
of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ by an orthogonal matrix
$\underline{\underline{R}} \in \mathcal{O}(K)$:
$\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{R}}$ and
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ produce the same
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$. This non-identifiability does not affect the
likelihood or the ELBO, but it does mean that the columns of
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ cannot be interpreted as TF programs without an
additional constraint. Standard remedies include constraining
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ to be lower-triangular, imposing column-wise
orthogonality, or applying a post-hoc principal-component rotation.

A second, subtler identifiability concern arises between the gene baseline
$\log \tilde{\mu}_g$ and the latent $z_{cg}$: both enter the conditional log-mean
additively. The zero-mean prior @eq-nbln-z-prior anchors the cell-averaged
$z_{cg}$ at zero, breaking this redundancy at the population level. In practice,
monitoring $\frac{1}{C}\sum_c \langle z_{cg} \rangle_q$ during training and
verifying it remains close to zero is a sufficient check.

## Variational inference {#sec-nbln-elbo}

We now turn to inference. The model has continuous global parameters
$\underline{\theta} = \{\tilde{\mu}_g, r_g, \underline{\underline{W}},
\underline{d}\}$ and one per-cell continuous latent $\underline{z}_c$. We
estimate all global parameters by maximizing a single-sample evidence lower
bound (ELBO), with $\underline{z}_c$ treated as a variational latent.

### ELBO

Choose a variational posterior factored across cells:

$$
q(\underline{\underline{Z}}) =
\prod_{c=1}^C q(\underline{z}_c) =
\prod_{c=1}^C
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{m}_c,
                   \underline{\underline{S}}_c\right),
$${#eq-nbln-q-form}

where $\underline{m}_c \in \mathbb{R}^G$ and $\underline{\underline{S}}_c$ is a
covariance matrix whose parameterization we discuss in @sec-nbln-amortization.
The ELBO decomposes additively across cells:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\underline{\theta}, \phi) =
\sum_{c=1}^C
\left[
\left\langle
    \sum_{g=1}^G \log
    \text{NB}\!\left(u_{cg} \mid \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}},\, r_g\right)
\right\rangle_{q(\underline{z}_c)}
- \text{KL}\!\left(q(\underline{z}_c)\,\|\,
                   p(\underline{z}_c)\right)
\right].
$${#eq-nbln-elbo}

### Closed-form KL term

Both $q(\underline{z}_c)$ and $p(\underline{z}_c)$ are Gaussian, so the KL has
the standard closed form

$$
\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\underline{m}_c, \underline{\underline{S}}_c)\,
                 \|\,\mathcal{N}(0, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})\right)
=
\frac{1}{2}\!\left[
    \text{tr}\!\left(\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}
                      \underline{\underline{S}}_c\right)
    + \underline{m}_c^\top \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1} \underline{m}_c
    - G
    + \log\frac{|\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}|}{|\underline{\underline{S}}_c|}
\right].
$${#eq-nbln-kl}

The Woodbury identities @eq-nbln-woodbury and @eq-nbln-logdet make this term
tractable at $O(K^2 G)$ per cell.

### Reparameterized Monte Carlo for the likelihood term

The expected log-likelihood does not have a closed form, but its gradient is
estimated cheaply by reparameterized sampling. Letting
$\underline{\underline{L}}_c$ be a Cholesky-like factor satisfying
$\underline{\underline{L}}_c \underline{\underline{L}}_c^\top =
\underline{\underline{S}}_c$, we draw

$$
\underline{z}_c =
\underline{m}_c + \underline{\underline{L}}_c \underline{\epsilon}_c,
\qquad
\underline{\epsilon}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \underline{\underline{I}}_G),
$${#eq-nbln-reparam}

and approximate the inner expectation by a single sample (or a small number of
samples). The gradient flows through the linear-in-$z$ term of the failure
logits @eq-nbln-failure-logit without difficulty.

### Log-concavity of the per-cell posterior

The conditional log-likelihood @eq-nbln-conditional-nb is concave in
$\underline{z}_c$. Differentiating $\log\,\text{NB}(u; \mu e^z, r)$ twice with
respect to $z$ gives

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \log\,\text{NB}(u; \mu e^z, r)}{\partial z^2}
= -(u + r)\, p(z)\, \bigl(1 - p(z)\bigr),
$${#eq-nbln-loglik-curvature}

with $p(z)$ defined in @eq-nbln-pg-z. This is strictly negative for
$u, r, \mu > 0$. Combined with the (negative) Gaussian prior log-density,
the per-cell log-posterior $\log \pi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{u}_c)$
has Hessian

$$
\nabla^2_{\underline{z}_c}
\log \pi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{u}_c)
= -\text{diag}\!\left(
    (u_{cg} + r_g)\, p_g(z_{cg})\, (1 - p_g(z_{cg}))
\right)_{g=1}^G
- \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1},
$${#eq-nbln-posterior-hessian}

which is strictly negative definite. The per-cell posterior over
$\underline{z}_c$ is therefore log-concave. This has two practical consequences:

1. The MAP solution exists, is unique, and can be found by Newton iteration
   with quadratic local convergence.
2. The Laplace approximation around the MAP is well-defined and well-behaved.

The log-concavity result @eq-nbln-posterior-hessian opens two distinct
inference paths, both of which scribe already supports for the
Poisson-LogNormal of @sec-poisson-lognormal. Path A — amortized stochastic
variational inference — replaces the per-cell variational parameters with the
output of a shared encoder network and optimizes the ELBO by stochastic
gradient descent. Path B — Laplace-approximated marginal-likelihood
inference — solves for the per-cell MAP by Newton iteration and maximizes the
resulting Laplace-approximated marginal likelihood with respect to the global
parameters. We derive each in turn.

### Path A: amortized stochastic variational inference {#sec-nbln-svi}

In the SVI path, the per-cell variational parameters
$(\underline{m}_c, \underline{\underline{S}}_c)$ are produced by a shared
encoder network $f_\phi$ from the observed counts:

$$
\bigl(\underline{m}_c,\ \log \underline{s}_c\bigr) =
f_\phi(\underline{u}_c).
$${#eq-nbln-encoder}

This **amortization** replaces $C \cdot 2G$ free variational parameters with
the (much smaller) parameter set $\phi$ of the encoder, and is what enables
the model to scale to single-cell datasets with
$C \sim 10^4$–$10^6$ cells.

#### Posterior covariance parameterization {#sec-nbln-amortization}

The variational covariance $\underline{\underline{S}}_c$ admits three standard
choices:

| Option | Form | Cost per cell | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean-field | $\text{diag}(\underline{s}_c^2)$ | $O(G)$ | Cheapest; ignores posterior cross-gene correlations |
| Low-rank | $\underline{\underline{V}}_c \underline{\underline{V}}_c^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{t}_c)$ | $O(GK')$ | Richer; encoder outputs $\underline{\underline{V}}_c$ and $\underline{t}_c$ |
| Full Cholesky | $\underline{\underline{L}}_c \underline{\underline{L}}_c^\top$ | $O(G^2)$ | Infeasible at scale |

The mean-field option is recommended as a starting point: the **prior**
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ already encodes the cross-gene correlation
structure of interest, so the additional flexibility of a structured posterior
is often unnecessary. The low-rank option becomes useful when the prior
covariance is misspecified or when the data carry strong residual correlation
beyond the $K$ regulatory components encoded by
$\underline{\underline{W}}$.

#### Encoder architecture

A small multi-layer perceptron with two or three hidden layers suffices in
practice. The input is either the raw count vector $\underline{u}_c$ or a
log-normalized variant $\log\!\bigl(1 + u_{cg} \cdot 10^4 / \ell_c\bigr)$;
the output layer has dimension $2G$ for the mean-field option, concatenating
$\underline{m}_c$ and $\log \underline{s}_c$. For $G \sim 2\,000$ this is
unproblematic on modern hardware. For substantially larger $G$, a bottleneck
architecture or a restriction to highly variable genes is appropriate.

#### Mini-batch ELBO and Adam optimization

The factorization @eq-nbln-elbo over cells admits unbiased mini-batch
estimates of the ELBO. For a mini-batch
$\mathcal{B} \subset \{1, \ldots, C\}$:

$$
\hat{\mathcal{L}}(\underline{\theta}, \phi) =
\frac{C}{|\mathcal{B}|}
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{B}}
\left[
\sum_{g=1}^G
\log \text{NB}\!\left(u_{cg} \mid \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}^{(1)}},\, r_g\right)
- \text{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{u}_c)\,
                   \|\, p(\underline{z}_c)\right)
\right],
$${#eq-nbln-svi-minibatch}

with $\underline{z}_c^{(1)}$ a single reparameterized sample from $q_\phi$
according to @eq-nbln-reparam, and the KL term given in closed form by
@eq-nbln-kl. Both the global parameters $\underline{\theta}$ and the encoder
weights $\phi$ are updated jointly by Adam.

### Path B: Laplace-approximated marginal-likelihood inference {#sec-nbln-laplace}

The log-concavity result @eq-nbln-posterior-hessian permits an alternative
path that bypasses the encoder entirely. Rather than amortizing the
posterior through a neural network, we approximate it cell-by-cell using a
Laplace expansion around the maximum a posteriori (MAP) point
$\underline{z}_c^*$:

$$
q_{\text{Lap}}(\underline{z}_c) =
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
    \underline{z}_c^*,\
    \bigl(-\underline{\underline{H}}_c^*\bigr)^{-1}
\right),
\qquad
\underline{\underline{H}}_c^* =
\nabla^2_{\underline{z}_c}
\log \pi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{u}_c)
\Big|_{\underline{z}_c = \underline{z}_c^*}.
$${#eq-nbln-laplace-form}

The corresponding Laplace-approximated marginal log-likelihood — the standard
result obtained by quadratic Taylor expansion of the integrand around its
mode and Gaussian integration of the residual — is

$$
\log \pi_{\text{Lap}}(\underline{u}_c \mid \underline{\theta})
=
\log \pi(\underline{u}_c, \underline{z}_c^* \mid \underline{\theta})
+ \frac{G}{2} \log(2\pi)
- \frac{1}{2} \log \bigl|-\underline{\underline{H}}_c^*\bigr|.
$${#eq-nbln-laplace-marginal}

Inference then alternates between (i) an **inner step** that solves for
$\underline{z}_c^*$ per cell, and (ii) an **outer step** that updates the
global parameters $\underline{\theta}$ to maximize the sum of
@eq-nbln-laplace-marginal over cells.

#### Inner step: Newton iteration for $\underline{z}_c^*$

The per-cell objective is the unnormalized log-posterior

$$
\Phi_c(\underline{z}_c)
=
\sum_{g=1}^G
\log \text{NB}\!\left(u_{cg} \mid \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}},\, r_g\right)
- \frac{1}{2}\,
  \underline{z}_c^\top
  \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}
  \underline{z}_c.
$${#eq-nbln-laplace-objective}

Differentiating @eq-nbln-laplace-objective once gives the gradient

$$
\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c(\underline{z}_c) =
\underline{u}_c -
(\underline{u}_c + \underline{r}) \odot
    \bigl(\underline{1} - \underline{p}(\underline{z}_c)\bigr)
- \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1} \underline{z}_c,
$${#eq-nbln-laplace-grad}

with $p_g(z_{cg}) = r_g / (r_g + \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}})$ and $\odot$ the
element-wise product. The Hessian is given by @eq-nbln-posterior-hessian.
The Newton update is

$$
\underline{z}_c \leftarrow
\underline{z}_c -
\bigl(\underline{\underline{H}}_c\bigr)^{-1}
\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c(\underline{z}_c).
$${#eq-nbln-newton-step}

By the strict log-concavity of $\Phi_c$, this iteration converges
quadratically to the unique MAP from any reasonable initialization. We use
$\underline{z}_c^{(0)} = \underline{0}$ (the prior mean) by default, with a
relative-gradient stopping criterion
$\|\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c\| < \tau$ for a small tolerance $\tau$.

#### Newton step via double Woodbury

Naive evaluation of the Newton update @eq-nbln-newton-step requires the
inverse of a dense $G \times G$ Hessian, which is infeasible for
$G \sim 10^3$ to $10^4$. The diagonal-plus-low-rank structure of
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}$ (itself a Woodbury expression,
@eq-nbln-woodbury) propagates to a diagonal-plus-low-rank Hessian that
admits a second Woodbury inversion. Concretely, write

$$
-\underline{\underline{H}}_c =
\text{diag}(\underline{a}_c) + \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1},
\qquad
a_{cg} = (u_{cg} + r_g)\,
         p_g(z_{cg})\,
         \bigl(1 - p_g(z_{cg})\bigr),
$${#eq-nbln-hessian-decomp}

and substitute the Woodbury form @eq-nbln-woodbury for
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}$:

$$
-\underline{\underline{H}}_c
=
\underbrace{\text{diag}\!\bigl(\underline{a}_c +
                                \underline{d}^{\,-1}\bigr)}_{
                  \underline{\underline{A}}_c}
-
\underbrace{\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}\,
            \underline{\underline{W}}}_{
                  \underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}}\,
\underline{\underline{M}}^{-1}\,
\underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}^\top,
\qquad
\underline{\underline{M}} =
\underline{\underline{I}}_K +
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}.
$${#eq-nbln-hessian-woodbury}

This is again **diagonal plus rank-$K$**. Applying Woodbury a second time
gives the inverse in closed form:

$$
\bigl(-\underline{\underline{H}}_c\bigr)^{-1}
=
\underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}
+
\underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}\,
\underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}\,
\bigl(\underline{\underline{M}} -
      \underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}^\top
      \underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}
      \underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}\bigr)^{-1}\,
\underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}^\top\,
\underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}.
$${#eq-nbln-hessian-inverse}

The matrix-vector product
$\bigl(-\underline{\underline{H}}_c\bigr)^{-1} \underline{v}$
therefore costs $O(GK + K^3)$ per cell, dominated by a few matrix-vector
products with $\underline{\underline{W}}$ and a single $K \times K$
inversion. The full Newton iteration is
$O(N_{\text{Newton}}\, (GK + K^3))$ per cell, with $N_{\text{Newton}}$
typically $5$–$10$ for the local quadratic regime.

#### Log-determinant via the matrix-determinant lemma

The Laplace marginal @eq-nbln-laplace-marginal also requires
$\log \bigl|-\underline{\underline{H}}_c^*\bigr|$. Applying the
matrix-determinant lemma to the form @eq-nbln-hessian-woodbury:

$$
\log \bigl|-\underline{\underline{H}}_c\bigr|
=
\log \bigl|\underline{\underline{A}}_c\bigr|
+
\log
\bigl|\underline{\underline{M}} -
      \underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}^\top
      \underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}
      \underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}\bigr|
-
\log \bigl|\underline{\underline{M}}\bigr|.
$${#eq-nbln-logdet-woodbury}

The first and third terms are scalar reductions over $G$ and a
$K \times K$ determinant respectively, each $O(G + K^3)$. The middle
term requires forming the $K \times K$ matrix
$\underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}^\top
\underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}
\underline{\underline{\widetilde{W}}}$, whose entries are inner products
of length-$G$ vectors weighted by the diagonal
$\underline{\underline{A}}_c^{-1}$ — a cost of $O(G K^2)$, plus a single
$K \times K$ determinant at $O(K^3)$. The dominant per-iteration cost
is therefore $O(G K^2 + K^3)$, breaking down as: each matrix-vector
product through $\bigl(-\underline{\underline{H}}_c\bigr)^{-1}$ at
$O(G K + K^3)$, multiplied by a constant number of Krylov / Newton
matvecs per inner step, plus a single $O(G K^2 + K^3)$ log-determinant
formation. The complete inner step (Newton iterations + log-determinant
+ Laplace marginal) is therefore
$O\!\bigl(N_{\text{Newton}}\,(G K^2 + K^3)\bigr)$ per cell, dominated by
the log-determinant on each Newton step.

#### Outer step: stochastic gradient on the Laplace marginal

The outer optimization maximizes the sum of Laplace marginals over cells:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{Lap}}(\underline{\theta}) =
\sum_{c=1}^C
\log \pi_{\text{Lap}}(\underline{u}_c \mid \underline{\theta}),
$${#eq-nbln-outer-objective}

with respect to
$\underline{\theta} = \{\tilde{\mu}_g, r_g, \underline{\underline{W}},
\underline{d}\}$, by mini-batched stochastic gradient. Two practical
choices govern how gradients flow through the inner step.

**Implicit-function gradients.** By the implicit function theorem, the
Newton-stationarity condition
$\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c(\underline{z}_c^*) = \underline{0}$ implies

$$
\frac{\partial \underline{z}_c^*}{\partial \underline{\theta}} =
-\bigl(\underline{\underline{H}}_c^*\bigr)^{-1}
\frac{\partial}{\partial \underline{\theta}}
\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c
\bigg|_{\underline{z}_c^*}.
$${#eq-nbln-ift}

This avoids backpropagation through the Newton iteration itself and reuses
the Hessian inverse @eq-nbln-hessian-inverse already computed in the inner
step.

**Unrolled differentiation.** Run a fixed number of Newton steps and let
automatic differentiation propagate gradients through the unrolled
iteration. Simpler to implement, sometimes preferred when the
implicit-function solve introduces additional numerical error.

In practice, the implicit-function route scales better at large $C$ and is
the route adopted by the PLN Laplace path in scribe.

#### Algorithm summary

The complete Laplace-EM scheme is summarized below. The notation $c \in
\mathcal{B}$ denotes iteration over cells in a mini-batch.

1. **Initialize** the global parameters
   $\underline{\theta} = \{\tilde{\mu}_g, r_g, \underline{\underline{W}},
   \underline{d}\}$ (e.g., method-of-moments fit on the empirical mean and
   variance per gene; $\underline{\underline{W}} = \underline{\underline{0}}$
   gives a clean NBDM starting point).
2. **Repeat** until convergence:
   1. Sample mini-batch $\mathcal{B} \subset \{1, \ldots, C\}$.
   2. **Inner step** (per cell $c \in \mathcal{B}$):
      initialize $\underline{z}_c^{(0)} = \underline{0}$;
      iterate the Newton update @eq-nbln-newton-step using
      @eq-nbln-hessian-inverse for the Hessian inverse;
      stop when
      $\|\nabla_{\underline{z}_c} \Phi_c\|_\infty < \tau$.
      Record $\underline{z}_c^*$ and
      $\log \bigl|-\underline{\underline{H}}_c^*\bigr|$ from
      @eq-nbln-logdet-woodbury.
   3. **Compute** the Laplace marginal @eq-nbln-laplace-marginal for each
      cell in the mini-batch.
   4. **Outer step**: take an Adam step on $\underline{\theta}$ using the
      gradient of @eq-nbln-outer-objective restricted to $\mathcal{B}$,
      with implicit-function or unrolled gradient flow through
      $\underline{z}_c^*$.

### Comparison and recommendation {#sec-nbln-inference-tradeoff}

| Property | Path A (SVI) | Path B (Laplace) |
|---|---|---|
| Per-cell posterior approximation | Amortized Gaussian via encoder | Local Gaussian at the MAP |
| Approximation quality | Limited by encoder capacity and amortization gap | Sharper at the MAP; covariance from local curvature |
| Per-cell training cost | One forward pass + one reparam sample | $N_{\text{Newton}}$ Newton steps with double-Woodbury |
| Hyperparameters | Encoder architecture, learning rate, batch size | Newton tolerance, max iterations, learning rate |
| Adequacy diagnostic | IWBO bound tightness | Symmetry of Laplace residual; IWBO post-hoc |
| Scribe infrastructure | PLN VAE pipeline | PLN Newton-with-Woodbury solver |

The two paths are complementary, not competitive. By analogy with the
Poisson-LogNormal experience (@sec-poisson-lognormal), the SVI path is
preferred during model development and large-scale exploration, while the
Laplace path is preferred for final fits where posterior approximation
quality is at stake. Both produce parameter estimates that can be
cross-validated by switching to the other path on the same data and
checking for agreement on global parameters.

## Composition sampling for compositional differential expression {#sec-nbln-composition}

The compositional differential-expression framework of @sec-diffexp-nonparametric
operates on samples drawn from the posterior predictive distribution of the
composition $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \underline{\lambda}^{(c)} / \sum_g
\lambda_g^{(c)}$. Under the NBDM model with shared $p$, the composition is
drawn from a Dirichlet,
$\underline{\rho} \sim \text{Dir}(\underline{r})$, by virtue of the
Gamma-Dirichlet factorization. We now show how this composition is sampled
under the NB-LogNormal, and we recover the Dirichlet form in the
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} \to 0$ limit.

### The Gamma-Dirichlet factorization breaks {#sec-nbln-factorization-breaks}

Conditional on $\underline{z}_c$, the latent rates $\lambda_{cg}$ are
independent Gamma random variables with **gene-specific** rates

$$
\beta_{cg}(\underline{z}_c) =
\frac{r_g}{\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}}.
$${#eq-nbln-beta-gene-specific}

By the same argument as in @sec-hier-factorization-breaks, the joint
density of the $\lambda_{cg}$ does **not** factor into a marginal distribution
over the total rate $\Lambda_c = \sum_g \lambda_{cg}$ and a Dirichlet
distribution over the proportions. The exponential cross-term

$$
\exp\!\left(-\Lambda_c \sum_{g=1}^G \beta_{cg}(\underline{z}_c)\,
                                    \rho_{cg}\right)
$${#eq-nbln-cross-term}

couples $\Lambda_c$ and $\underline{\rho}_c$ through the gene-specific rates.
The Dirichlet form is recovered if and only if all $\beta_{cg}$ are equal,
which under @eq-nbln-beta-gene-specific requires both $r_g / \mu_{cg}$ to be
equal across genes (the original shared-$p$ assumption of NBDM) and
$z_{cg}$ to be equal across genes (which the Gaussian prior, being mean-zero
with general $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, does not enforce).

Compositions under the NB-LogNormal therefore depend on $\underline{z}_c$ and
must be sampled accordingly.

### Generalized composition sampling

We use the same Gamma-based generalized procedure introduced in
@sec-hier-gamma-sampling, adapted to the NB-LogNormal generative form.
Specifically, for each posterior sample $s$ of the global parameters and each
posterior or prior draw of $\underline{z}_c^{(s)}$:

1. Draw unit-rate Gamma variates independently per gene:
   $$
   \gamma_g^{(s)} \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g^{(s)}, 1),
   \qquad g = 1, \ldots, G.
   $${#eq-nbln-comp-gamma-draw}

2. Scale by the gene-specific factor:
   $$
   \tilde{\lambda}_{cg}^{(s)} =
   \gamma_g^{(s)} \cdot
   \frac{\mu_{cg}^{(s)}\, e^{z_{cg}^{(s)}}}{r_g^{(s)}}.
   $${#eq-nbln-comp-scaling}

3. Normalize to the simplex:
   $$
   \rho_{cg}^{(s)} =
   \frac{\tilde{\lambda}_{cg}^{(s)}}
        {\sum_{j=1}^G \tilde{\lambda}_{cj}^{(s)}}.
   $${#eq-nbln-comp-normalize}

By the change-of-variables argument in @sec-hier-gamma-sampling, the
$\tilde{\lambda}_{cg}^{(s)}$ have the same distribution as the conditional
Gamma variates from @eq-nbln-lambda-gamma. The composition
$\underline{\rho}_c^{(s)}$ is therefore the compositional restriction of the
NB-LogNormal latent rate, conditional on the sampled $\underline{z}_c^{(s)}$.

### Reduction to the NBDM Dirichlet form when $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = 0$

When $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{0}}$ we have
$\underline{z}_c = \underline{0}$ for all cells, and the composition reduces to

$$
\rho_{cg}^{(s)} =
\frac{\gamma_g^{(s)} \cdot \mu_{cg}^{(s)} / r_g^{(s)}}
     {\sum_{j} \gamma_j^{(s)} \cdot \mu_{cj}^{(s)} / r_j^{(s)}}.
$${#eq-nbln-comp-no-z}

If, additionally, the baseline mean factorizes as $\mu_{cg} = \ell_c\,
\tilde{\mu}_g$ (@eq-nbln-mean-decomposition) and the gene-specific ratio
$\tilde{\mu}_g / r_g$ is the constant $(1 - \hat{p}) / \hat{p}$ that defines
the shared-$p$ NBDM (cf. @sec-dirichlet-multinomial), then the gene-independent
factor $\ell_c\, (1 - \hat{p}) / \hat{p}$ cancels between numerator and
denominator and the composition reduces to

$$
\rho_{cg}^{(s)} =
\frac{\gamma_g^{(s)}}{\sum_j \gamma_j^{(s)}},
$${#eq-nbln-comp-dirichlet}

with $\gamma_g^{(s)} \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g^{(s)}, 1)$ — the Dirichlet sampling
form @eq-hier-summary-dirichlet recovered exactly. $\square$

### Pipeline integration

The downstream centered-log-ratio differential-expression pipeline of
@sec-diffexp-nonparametric operates on samples
$\underline{\rho}_c^{(s)}$ regardless of how they were generated. The
NB-LogNormal therefore plugs into the existing pipeline through the modified
sampler @eq-nbln-comp-gamma-draw–@eq-nbln-comp-normalize, with the additional
input that posterior or prior samples of $\underline{z}_c$ must be supplied
together with samples of the global parameters.

In practice, posterior predictive checks evaluate the empirical zero-fraction,
mean, and variance per gene against draws from the marginal $\pi(u_{cg})$,
which is obtained by drawing $\underline{z}_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ and then $u_{cg} \sim
\text{NB}(\mu_{cg} e^{z_{cg}}, r_g)$.

## Mathematical summary {#sec-nbln-summary}

We collect the key results of this section.

**Generative model.** For $c = 1, \ldots, C$ and $g = 1, \ldots, G$:

$$
\underline{z}_c \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
\qquad
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d}),
$${#eq-nbln-summary-prior}

$$
\lambda_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{Gamma}\!\left(r_g,\
    \frac{r_g}{\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}}\right),
\qquad
u_{cg} \mid \lambda_{cg} \sim
\text{Poisson}(\lambda_{cg}).
$${#eq-nbln-summary-gen}

**Marginalized form.** After integrating out $\lambda_{cg}$ via Gamma-Poisson
conjugacy:

$$
u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{NB}\!\left(\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}},\ r_g\right).
$${#eq-nbln-summary-nb}

**Failure logits.** Linear in $\underline{z}_c$:

$$
\log\frac{1 - p_g(z_{cg})}{p_g(z_{cg})} =
\log \mu_{cg} + z_{cg} - \log r_g.
$${#eq-nbln-summary-logits}

**Posterior log-concavity.** The per-cell log-posterior over
$\underline{z}_c$ has Hessian

$$
\nabla^2_{\underline{z}_c} \log
\pi(\underline{z}_c \mid \underline{u}_c)
= -\text{diag}\bigl((u_{cg} + r_g)\, p_g(z_{cg})\,
                    (1 - p_g(z_{cg}))\bigr)
- \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1},
$${#eq-nbln-summary-hessian}

which is strictly negative definite.

**Limiting cases.**

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} \to \underline{\underline{0}}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
u_{cg} \sim \text{NB}(\mu_{cg}, r_g)
\quad \text{(independent NB; reduces to NBDM under shared-$p$)},
$${#eq-nbln-summary-limit-nbdm}

$$
r_g \to \infty
\;\Longrightarrow\;
u_{cg} \mid \underline{z}_c \sim
\text{Poisson}\!\left(\mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}}\right)
\quad \text{(PLN, @sec-poisson-lognormal)}.
$${#eq-nbln-summary-limit-pln}

**Composition sampling.** For PPC and compositional DE:

$$
\gamma_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, 1),
\qquad
\rho_{cg} =
\frac{\gamma_g \cdot \mu_{cg}\, e^{z_{cg}} / r_g}
     {\sum_j \gamma_j \cdot \mu_{cj}\, e^{z_{cj}} / r_j}.
$${#eq-nbln-summary-composition}

When $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{0}}$ and the
shared-$p$ NBDM constraint $\tilde{\mu}_g / r_g = (1 - \hat{p}) / \hat{p}$
holds, this reduces to $\underline{\rho} \sim \text{Dir}(\underline{r})$.
